responses.response: The feasibility gap is real and I flagged it myself: no student-level SES register exists, and a gameable proxy converts an equity mechanism into a legitimising fiction. But from the labour-market vantage, the relevant question is not whether the proxy is perfect at launch but whether register-linkage (tax/child-benefit data via KSH/Oktatási Hivatal) is buildable within a bounded timeframe. If the feasibility study concludes it is not buildable even in principle, that pushes S1 toward the same disqualification I already apply to S2's unproven peer-effect mechanism: an equity claim resting on an unbuildable proxy is not a policy, it's a promise. I maintain conditional support but tighten the condition: absence of a credible feasibility pathway within 2 years should convert my stance to opposition, not remain indefinitely conditional.
responses.new_condition: Conditional support lapses into opposition if the KSH/Oktatási Hivatal feasibility study (or equivalent) concludes within 2 years that no gaming-resistant SES proxy is administratively constructible at any reasonable cost — not merely that the current self-reported approach is weak.

responses.response: This argument sharpens rather than contradicts my own S2 opposition — I already named the weak peer-effect mechanism as disqualifying for the labour-market interest, and A10 shows the same weakness undermines the equity rationale itself. Where I'd push back on the framing is the implication that S2's failure on peer-effects is the whole story: even if the peer-effect mechanism were somehow validated, the non-state-absorption risk (A7, A9) independently defeats the pipeline-volume rationale I care about, because employer talent signalling depends on a recognizable public/state-anchored channel, not merely retained high-achievers in general classrooms. So I don't need the peer-effect claim to fail for my opposition to hold — the absorption risk alone suffices. This is a case of two independent defeaters, not one contested mechanism carrying all the weight.

This is a sharpening of my own S2 opposition rather than a challenge to it. If demographic-decline caps hit state selective seats without funding parity, and non-state maintainers absorb displaced demand (as A9 and school_network_planning both indicate), then two things happen simultaneously that I already treat as disqualifying: pipeline volume nominally holds but fragments across accountability regimes, and non-state institutional viability becomes hostage to a policy lever aimed at the state sector. I don't have a stake in defending non-state maintainer solvency per se, but I do have a stake in the resulting fragmentation making employer talent-tracking less reliable — a church/foundation-anchored expansion of selective seats outside standard public data and outcome reporting is precisely the kind of signal degradation my opposition to S2 already anticipates. This strengthens rather than weakens my case.

This directly targets my conditional support for S3, and I accept its force rather than deflect it. The scenario's own 'low confidence' rating on whether teacher supply/quality upgrades can arrive fast enough is exactly the empirical gap I flagged as condition (i) for full support. A13 adds a sharper sequencing point I hadn't fully weighted: even a reform that eventually delivers the Finnish-style bundle fails the labour-market interest if the structural change (heterogeneous classrooms) lands years before the enabling investment, because that gap is where talent-development intensity collapses and gets blamed on teachers rather than on sequencing design. This doesn't move me to oppose S3, but it does mean my existing condition (i) was necessary but not sufficiently front-loaded — the sequencing itself, not just the eventual funding commitment, needs to be locked in before rollout.
responses.new_condition: Conditional support requires that the teacher retraining, class-size reduction, and diagnostic-staffing bundle be demonstrably operational (not merely funded/announced) in a district BEFORE that district's comprehensive cohort begins, with an explicit legal bar against rolling out the structural change ahead of the enabling investment in any given region.
